PARAGRAPH 1
Creating a robust Python function to filter historical dates by century requires careful attention to mathematical definitions and computational efficiency. Many researchers encounter confusion regarding how centuries are numbered versus how years are calculated. The standard historical convention dictates that the first century spans years one through one hundred, while the second century covers years one hundred and one through two hundred. Consequently, the nineteenth century includes the years one thousand eight hundred and one through one thousand nine hundred. This means the year one thousand nine hundred belongs to the nineteenth century, whereas the year one thousand nine hundred and one starts the twentieth century. To implement this logic correctly, we must calculate the century of any given year using integer arithmetic. Specifically, the century number for a specific year can be derived by taking the year, subtracting one, dividing by one hundred, and then adding one. This formula ensures that years ending in zero correctly map to the preceding century number. For instance, the year one thousand nine hundred yields a century value of nineteen, while the year one thousand nine hundred and one yields a century value of twenty. Our function will accept a list of date strings formatted as year, month, and day separated by hyphens, along with an integer target century. We will iterate through each date string, extract the year component, and apply our century calculation logic. If the calculated century matches the target integer, we will include that date string in our result list. This approach avoids converting strings to complex date objects, which can be computationally expensive when processing millions of records. By working directly with string slicing and integer conversion, we maintain high performance. The input list might contain dates spanning several millennia, so efficiency is paramount. We must ensure our code handles edge cases gracefully, such as empty lists or centuries that contain no matching dates in the dataset. Furthermore, we should validate that the input strings follow the expected format to prevent runtime errors during the year extraction phase. Although the prompt assumes valid input, adding a simple check for string length or character type can prevent crashes in production environments. The function will return a new list containing only the filtered dates, preserving their original string format. This design choice ensures that downstream processes receive data in the exact format they expect without requiring additional parsing or reformatting steps. The solution is designed to be scalable, meaning it will perform well even as the dataset grows to include hundreds of thousands of entries. Memory usage remains low because we process items sequentially and only store the matching results. We do not create unnecessary intermediate data structures or copies of the input list
